name: Cognitive biases
description: Systematic errors in human judgement and decision-making — what each bias is, with a quick everyday example. Flip cards plus a few 'which bias is this?' multiple-choice cards.
primer:
A **cognitive bias** is a systematic (predictable, repeatable) error in judgement — not a random slip. Each card tests one. A compact glossary:

**Memory & belief**
- **Confirmation bias** — seeking, favouring and recalling evidence that fits what you already believe, while discounting what contradicts it.
- **Hindsight bias** — once an outcome is known, believing you 'knew it all along' and that it was more predictable than it was.
- **Recency bias** — over-weighting the most recent events when judging a trend.
- **Negativity bias** — bad experiences carry more psychological weight than equally strong good ones.

**Social & self**
- **Dunning–Kruger effect** — low competence leads people to overrate their ability (the same gaps hide the mistakes); experts may underrate themselves.
- **Fundamental attribution error** — blaming *others'* behaviour on their character, but excusing *your own* with circumstances.
- **Self-serving bias** — claiming successes as your own doing, blaming failures on outside factors.
- **Bandwagon effect** — adopting a belief or behaviour because many others have, not because of evidence.
- **Halo effect** — one positive trait (e.g. attractiveness) colours your whole judgement of someone.
- **Optimism bias** — overestimating your own chances of good outcomes and underestimating bad ones.
- **Negativity bias** *(also belief)* — see above.
- **In-group bias** — favouring members of your own group over outsiders.
- **Overconfidence effect** — your stated certainty exceeds your actual accuracy.

**Decision & probability**
- **Anchoring** — over-relying on the first number/value seen, so later estimates stay too close to it.
- **Availability heuristic** — judging likelihood by how easily examples come to mind, so vivid/recent events feel more frequent.
- **Sunk cost fallacy** — continuing a losing course because of resources already spent, rather than future value.
- **Loss aversion** — a loss feels larger than an equal-sized gain, so you work harder to avoid losses.
- **Framing effect** — reacting differently to identical facts depending on positive vs negative wording (90% survival vs 10% mortality).
- **Survivorship bias** — drawing conclusions only from cases that passed a selection filter, ignoring the dropouts.
- **Gambler's fallacy** — believing past *independent* random outcomes make others 'due'. Independent events have no memory; nothing is ever due.
- **Status quo bias** — preferring things to stay the same, treating change as a loss.
- **Base rate neglect** — ignoring how common something is overall, over-weighting specific case details (why a positive test for a rare disease is less alarming than it feels).
- **Planning fallacy** — chronically underestimating how long a task will take, even given past overruns.

For the 'identify the bias' cards, match the scenario to the definition above; the wrong options are other biases in this list.

cards.definition: The tendency to seek, interpret and remember information that confirms what you already believe, while discounting evidence against it. Example: convinced a stock will rise, you read only bullish analysis and skip the warnings.
cards.summary: Favouring evidence that fits your existing belief.

cards.definition: Relying too heavily on the first piece of information (the 'anchor') when making estimates, so later judgements stay too close to it. Example: a shop shows '€200, now €120' and €120 feels cheap only because of the €200 anchor.
cards.summary: First number seen drags your estimate toward it.

cards.definition: Judging how likely something is by how easily examples come to mind, so vivid or recent events seem more frequent than they are. Example: after seeing news of a plane crash, you overestimate the risk of flying.
cards.summary: Easy-to-recall events feel more probable.

cards.definition: After an outcome is known, believing you 'knew it all along' and that it was more predictable than it really was. Example: after an election result, insisting the outcome was obvious from the start.
cards.summary: Seeing a past event as having been predictable.

cards.definition: People with low competence in an area overestimate their ability, because the same gaps prevent them from seeing their mistakes; experts may underrate themselves. Example: a beginner programmer who thinks one tutorial made them an expert.
cards.summary: The unskilled overrate themselves; experts underrate themselves.

cards.definition: Continuing a losing course of action because of resources already spent, rather than judging future value. Example: sitting through a bad film 'because I paid for the ticket'.
cards.summary: Throwing good money after bad to justify past spending.

cards.definition: Losses feel psychologically larger than equivalent gains, so people work harder to avoid losing than to win. Example: the pain of losing €50 outweighs the pleasure of finding €50.
cards.summary: Losses hurt more than equal gains please.

cards.definition: Reacting differently to the same facts depending on how they are presented (positively vs negatively). Example: '90% survival rate' sounds far better than '10% mortality rate', though they are identical.
cards.summary: The same information feels different depending on wording.

cards.definition: Drawing conclusions only from cases that 'survived' a selection process, ignoring those that dropped out. Example: studying only successful start-up founders and missing the many with the same habits who failed.
cards.summary: Looking only at winners and ignoring the silent losers.

cards.definition: Attributing others' behaviour to their character while excusing your own with circumstances. Example: a colleague is late because they're 'lazy', but you were late because 'traffic was terrible'.
cards.summary: Blaming others' character, but your own situation.

cards.definition: Taking credit for successes (internal causes) but blaming failures on outside factors. Example: 'I aced the exam because I'm smart, but failed the other because the questions were unfair.'
cards.summary: Successes are mine; failures are someone else's fault.

cards.definition: Adopting beliefs or behaviours because many others have, regardless of the underlying evidence. Example: buying a product simply because it's trending and 'everyone has one'.
cards.summary: Believing or doing something because the crowd does.

cards.definition: Letting one positive trait colour your overall judgement of a person or thing. Example: assuming an attractive or charismatic candidate is also competent and honest.
cards.summary: One good trait makes everything else look good.

cards.definition: Believing that past independent random events change future probabilities. Example: after five reds on the roulette wheel, betting on black because it's 'due', though each spin is independent.
cards.summary: Thinking past chance outcomes make others 'due'.

cards.definition: Giving disproportionate weight to the most recent events when judging trends. Example: an investor expecting last month's market direction to simply continue.
cards.summary: Overweighting the latest information.

cards.definition: Overestimating the likelihood of good outcomes and underestimating bad ones for yourself. Example: believing you personally are less likely than average to get ill or have an accident.
cards.summary: Expecting good things to happen to you more than to others.

cards.definition: Preferring things to stay the same and treating any change as a loss, even when change would help. Example: keeping a worse default phone plan simply to avoid switching.
cards.summary: Defaulting to 'keep things as they are'.

cards.definition: Giving negative experiences more psychological weight than equally strong positive ones. Example: dwelling on one critical comment among ten praising ones.
cards.summary: Bad impressions stick harder than good ones.

cards.definition: Favouring members of your own group over outsiders in trust, judgement and reward. Example: rating ideas from your own team as better simply because they're 'ours'.
cards.summary: Favouring 'us' over 'them'.

cards.definition: Excessive confidence in the accuracy of your own judgements, so your stated certainty exceeds your actual hit rate. Example: being '90% sure' of trivia answers that turn out right only 60% of the time.
cards.summary: Certainty that outruns accuracy.

cards.definition: Ignoring the general prevalence of something (the base rate) and over-weighting specific case details. Example: a positive test for a rare disease feels alarming until you account for how few people actually have it.
cards.summary: Ignoring how common something is in the first place.

cards.definition: Underestimating how long a task will take, even when past similar tasks ran over. Example: promising a report 'by Friday' that predictably slips to the following week.
cards.summary: Chronically underestimating time to finish.

cards.term: The gambler's fallacy correctly assumes that independent random events become 'due' to balance out.
cards.summary: False — independent events have no memory.
cards.definition: False. The gambler's fallacy is exactly the mistaken belief that past independent outcomes change future probabilities. Each spin or coin toss is independent, so nothing is ever 'due'.

cards.term: Loss aversion means a loss typically feels larger than a gain of the same size.
cards.summary: True — losses loom larger than equivalent gains.
cards.definition: True. Loss aversion is the tendency for losses to carry more psychological weight than gains of equal magnitude, which is why people often go to greater lengths to avoid a loss than to secure a comparable gain.

cards.term: Which bias describes seeking out only the evidence that supports what you already believe?
cards.sub: A) Confirmation bias    B) Availability heuristic    C) Anchoring bias    D) Status quo bias
cards.definition: Correct: A) Confirmation bias. It is the tendency to seek, favour and recall information consistent with existing beliefs. The availability heuristic is about ease of recall, anchoring about over-relying on a first value, and status quo bias about preferring no change.

cards.term: A friend is late and you decide they're lazy, but when you're late it's because of traffic. Which bias is this?
cards.sub: A) Self-serving bias    B) Fundamental attribution error    C) In-group bias    D) Halo effect
cards.definition: Correct: B) Fundamental attribution error. It is attributing others' behaviour to their character while explaining your own by the situation. Self-serving bias is about credit and blame for your own outcomes, in-group bias about favouring your group, and the halo effect about one trait colouring overall judgement.

cards.term: An investor refuses to sell a failing project 'because of all the money already poured in'. Which bias is this?
cards.sub: A) Loss aversion    B) Optimism bias    C) Sunk cost fallacy    D) Recency bias
cards.definition: Correct: C) Sunk cost fallacy. The decision is driven by resources already spent rather than future value. Loss aversion concerns weighting losses over gains, optimism bias over-expecting good outcomes, and recency bias over-weighting the latest events.

cards.term: Studying only successful founders and copying their habits, ignoring those who failed with the same habits, exemplifies which bias?
cards.sub: A) Bandwagon effect    B) Survivorship bias    C) Hindsight bias    D) Base rate neglect
cards.definition: Correct: B) Survivorship bias. Conclusions are drawn only from cases that passed a selection filter, ignoring the dropouts. The bandwagon effect is following the crowd, hindsight bias is seeing the past as predictable, and base rate neglect is ignoring overall prevalence.
